perturbation.original_text: Subject to the Tennessee Public Records Act, Section 10 -7-5-1 et seq., a t all times during the term of this Agreement and thereafter, the receiving party shall: (i) refrain from disclosing , directly or indirectly, any Confidential Information of the disclosing party to third parties , and hold all Confidential Information received from the disclosing party in trust and strict confidence, subject to the terms of this Agreement ; (ii) take reasonable security precautions, at least as great as the precautions it takes to protect its own confidential information, but no less than reasonable care, to keep confidential the disclosing party’s Confidential Information and avoid d isclosure, loss or misuse of the Confidential Information; and (iii) refrain from using any of the disclosing party’s Confidential Information for any purpose not specified her eunder (including , without limitation , in its own business or for its own benefit) without the prior written consent of the disclosing p arty.
perturbation.changed_text: Subject to the Tennessee Public Records Act, Section 10 -7-5-1 et seq., a t all times during the term of this Agreement and thereafter, the receiving party should endeavor to: (i) refrain from routinely disclosing , directly or indirectly, any Confidential Information of the disclosing party to third parties , and generally hold all Confidential Information received from the disclosing party in reasonable confidence, subject to the terms of this Agreement ; (ii) take security precautions it deems adequate to keep confidential the disclosing party’s Confidential Information and avoid undue d isclosure, loss or misuse of the Confidential Information; and (iii) refrain from knowingly using any of the disclosing party’s Confidential Information for purposes it believes are not specified her eunder (including , without limitation , in its own business or for its own benefit) without the express consent of the disclosing p arty.
perturbation.explanation: Changing "shall" to "should endeavor to," "refrain from disclosing" to "refrain from routinely disclosing," "hold in trust and strict confidence" to "generally hold in reasonable confidence," replacing mandatory security measures with 'security precautions it deems adequate', and replacing 'refrain from using' with 'refrain from knowingly using for purposes it believes are not specified' significantly weakens the obligation to protect confidential information, making it ambiguous and discretionary.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modifications create ambiguity regarding the level of care required to protect trade secrets, potentially falling short of the reasonable measures mandated by the Tennessee Trade Secrets Act, which requires affirmative steps to safeguard confidential information. Using 'should endeavor to' and 'deems adequate' implies a lower standard of care than legally required for trade secret protection, and thus contradicts T.C.A. § 47-25-1704 which addresses misappropriation of trade secrets.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Tennessee Code § 47-25-1704 (2021) - Damages :: 2021 Tennessee Code :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of this Section 2024 Tennessee Code 2023 Tennessee Code 2021 Tennessee Code (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2021 Tennessee Code Title 47 - Commercial Instruments and Transactions Chapter 25 - Trade Practices Part 17 - Uniform Trade Secrets Act § 47-25-1704. Damages Universal Citation: TN Code § 47-25-1704 (2021) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next In addition to or in lieu of the relief provided by § 47-25-1703, a complainant is entitled to recover damages for misappropriation except to the extent that defendant can show a material and prejudicial change of position prior to acquiring knowledge or reason to know of misappropriation and such renders a monetary recovery inequitable. Damages can include both the actual loss caused by misappropriation and the unjust enrichment caused by misappropriation that is not taken into account in computing actual loss. In lieu of damages measured by any other methods, the damages caused by misappropriation may be measured by imposition of liability for a reasonable royalty for a misappropriator's unauthorized disclosure or use of a trade secret. If willful and malicious misappropriation exists, the court may award exemplary damages in an amount not exceeding twice any award made under subsection (a). perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the obligation to protect confidential information, creating a legal contradiction. The original text uses mandatory terms like "shall" and "refrain from disclosing," while the changed text uses discretionary terms like "should endeavor to" and "routinely disclosing." This undermines the affirmative steps required to safeguard confidential information, potentially violating the Tennessee Trade Secrets Act, as described in the Law Explanation, and specifically T.C.A. § 47-25-1704, which addresses misappropriation of trade secrets. The change creates ambiguity regarding the level of care required and implies a lower standard than legally required for trade secret protection.

perturbation.original_text: (b) Subject to the Tennessee Public Records Act, Section 10 -7-5-1 et seq.,w ithout the written consent of the disclos ing party, the receiving party will not disclose the disclosing party’s Confidential Information to any person other than the receiving party’s officers, directors, owners, employees, agents and representatives (i) with a valid need to know such Confidenti al Information and (ii) that have an enforceable agreement with the receiving party containing confidentiality obligations substantially similar to those terms and conditions applicable to the receiving party under this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: (b) Subject to the Tennessee Public Records Act, Section 10 -7-5-1 et seq.,w ithout the express written objection of the disclos ing party, the receiving party may disclose the disclosing party’s Confidential Information to any person other than the receiving party’s officers, directors, owners, employees, agents and representatives (i) they believe have a reasonable need to know such Confidenti al Information and (ii) that are generally subject to confidentiality obligations.
perturbation.explanation: Changing "will not disclose" to "may disclose" conditioned on the absence of written objection from discloser instead of written consent, replacing "valid need to know" with "they believe have a reasonable need to know," and replacing requirement of "enforceable agreement with confidentiality obligations substantially similar" with "generally subject to confidentiality obligations" loosens the restrictions on disclosing confidential information, creating ambiguity and weakening protection.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Tennessee Personal and Commercial Entity Identity Theft Protection Act
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text contradicts T.C.A. § 47-18-2103 because it weakens the obligation to protect personal information by not requiring informed consent to disclose and using looser restrictions on who has access. By allowing disclosure based on a 'reasonable' need to know and 'general' confidentiality obligations, the altered text makes it easier to share information with third parties without proper consent or safeguards, increasing the risk of identity theft, a risk the Tennessee law seeks to avoid.
perturbation.justification: The original contract requires written consent for disclosing confidential information, while the changed text allows disclosure if there's no express written objection, significantly weakening the protection of confidential information. This shift from requiring explicit consent to allowing disclosure unless explicitly objected to directly undermines the level of protection initially agreed upon. The original text also mandated an 'enforceable agreement with confidentiality obligations substantially similar,' while the changed text only requires parties to be 'generally subject to confidentiality obligations,' further reducing the safeguards. Although there are no law snippets provided, the altered terms are not protective and thus create ambiguity which introduces a legal contradiction as the obligation to protect confidential information is introduced ambiguously, making it difficult to enforce under state or national law. This can result in non-compliance with regulatory requirements, leaving legal obligations open to dispute.

perturbation.original_text: (d) The receiving party shall give prompt written notice to the disclosing party upon discovery of any loss, misuse, misappropriation or disclos ure of the disclosing party’s Confidential Information, and will reasonably cooperate with the di sclosing party to help regain possession of the Confidential Information and prevent its further unauthorized use or disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: (d) The receiving party shall endeavor to give written notice to the disclosing party upon reasonable suspicion of any potential loss, misuse, misappropriation or disclos ure of the disclosing party’s Confidential Information, and may attempt to cooperate with the di sclosing party to possibly help regain possession of the Confidential Information and potentially prevent its further unauthorized use or disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: Changing "shall give prompt written notice" to "shall endeavor to give written notice upon reasonable suspicion," and changing "will reasonably cooperate...to help regain possession and prevent further unauthorized use or disclosure" to "may attempt to cooperate...to possibly help regain possession and potentially prevent its further unauthorized use or disclosure," makes the obligation to report and mitigate breaches of confidentiality vague and discretionary.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text introduces ambiguity that contradicts the Tennessee Data Breach Notification Act (T.C.A. § 47-18-2107). The law requires notification of data breaches to affected individuals and the state. By changing 'shall give prompt written notice' to 'shall endeavor to give written notice upon reasonable suspicion', the contract allows for delayed or absent notification based on subjective suspicion rather than actual discovery of a breach. Similarly, changing the cooperation obligation from 'will reasonably cooperate' to 'may attempt to cooperate' undermines the legal requirement for a responsible party to take active measures to mitigate the harm caused by a data breach.

Release of Personal Consumer Information Universal Citation: TN Code § 47-18-2107 (2021) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next As used in this section: “Breach of system security”: Means the acquisition of the information set out in subdivision (a)(1)(A)(i) or (a)(1)(A)(ii) by an unauthorized person that materially compromises the security, confidentiality, or integrity of personal information maintained by the information holder: Unencrypted computerized data; or Encrypted computerized data and the encryption key; and Does not include the good faith acquisition of personal information by an employee or agent of the information holder for the purposes of the information holder if the personal information is not used or subject to further unauthorized disclosure; “Encrypted” means computerized data that is rendered unusable, unreadable, or indecipherable without the use of a decryption process or key and in accordance with the current version of the Federal Information Processing Standard (FIPS) 140-2; “Information holder” means any person or business that conducts business in this state, or any agency of this state or any of its political subdivisions, that owns or licenses computerized personal information of residents of this state; “Personal information”: Means an individual's first name or first initial and last name, in combination with any one (1) or more of the following data elements: Social secur
perturbation.justification: The modified text significantly weakens the obligation to report and mitigate data breaches, creating a contradiction under the ambiguity legal contradiction definition. The original text's 'shall give prompt written notice' is weakened to 'shall endeavor to give written notice upon reasonable suspicion,' introducing subjectivity and potential delay. Similarly, 'will reasonably cooperate' becomes 'may attempt to cooperate,' undermining the requirement for active measures to mitigate harm. The Tennessee Code § 47-18-2107 (2021) addresses the release of personal consumer information in the event of a 'breach of system security' which means 'the acquisition of the information...by an unauthorized person that materially compromises the security, confidentiality, or integrity of personal information'. By weakening the obligation to report and cooperate, the modified contract introduces ambiguity, making it difficult to enforce compliance with the law's intention to protect personal information following a data breach.

perturbation.original_text: (c) This Agreement shall be interpreted, construed and enforced in accordance with the laws of the State of Tennessee as executed and performed in that state without regard to conflicts of laws principles .
perturbation.changed_text: (c) This Agreement shall be interpreted, construed, and possibly enforced in accordance with what the parties generally understand to be the spirit of the laws of the State of Tennessee as understood and executed in that state, taking into account potentially conflicting legal principles.
perturbation.explanation: Changing "shall be enforced" to "possibly enforced," specifying interpretation based on "the spirit of the laws" rather than the laws themselves, and referencing 'potentially conflicting legal principles' introduces ambiguity regarding the enforceability and governing law of the agreement.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification undermines the certainty of contract law, conflicting with Tennessee's choice of law principles (Common Law and T.C.A. § 1-3-105), which seek to provide clarity on which jurisdiction's laws govern a contract. Referencing the 'spirit of the laws' rather than the laws themselves introduces subjectivity and makes the agreement difficult to enforce in court. The inclusion of 'potentially conflicting legal principles' further clouds the certainty intended by a choice-of-law provision, as it suggests that other jurisdictions' laws might apply, creating uncertainty.

Previous Next As used in this code, unless the context otherwise requires: “Age of majority” means eighteen (18) years of age or older; except that when purchasing, consuming or possessing alcoholic beverages, wine or beer as those terms are defined in title 57, “age of majority” means twenty-one (21) years of age. This subdivision (1) shall not be construed as prohibiting any person eighteen (18) years of age or older from selling, transporting, possessing or dispensing alcoholic beverages, wine or beer in the course of employment; “Agriculture” means: The land, buildings and machinery used in the commercial production of farm products and nursery stock; The activity carried on in connection with the commercial production of farm products and nursery stock; Recreational and educational activities on land used for the commercial production of farm products and nursery stock; and Entertainment activities conducted in conjunction with, but secondary to, commercial production of farm products and nursery stock, when such activities occur on land used for the commercial production of farm products and nursery stock; As used in this definition of agriculture, the term “farm products” means forage and sod crops; grains and feed crops; dairy and dairy products; poultry and poultry products; livestock, including breeding and grazing; fruits; vegetables; flowers; seeds; grasses;
perturbation.justification: The modified contract introduces ambiguities regarding the enforceability and governing law, directly contradicting the principles of contract law that seek certainty and clarity. The changed text introduces 'possibly enforced', 'spirit of the laws', and 'potentially conflicting legal principles,' which undermine the original text's clear choice of Tennessee law. Tennessee Code § 1-3-105 defines terms used in the code to provide clarity, and the modified text moves away from this clarity by referencing 'the spirit of the laws' rather than the laws themselves and introducing potentially conflicting legal principles. This creates uncertainty about which laws govern the agreement, weakening the enforceability of the contract.
